title: Etsy Is In Its Flop Era, and Sellers Are Fleeing
gist: Longtime handmade Etsy sellers are fleeing the platform as AI-generated art and drop-shipped copies flood listings and erode trust, with the company's permissive 2024 AI policy and weak enforcement opening the door to human-moderated competitors like Fybe while Etsy's gross merchandise sales have declined since 2021.

insights.claim: AI-generated pet portraits are outranking and undercutting handmade listings in Etsy search results and selling for a fraction of the price, with buyers giving them great reviews indifferent to who or what made them.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Consumer indifference to provenance means AI content structurally wins on price, compressing margins for human makers.
insights.quote: They didn’t really know or care who made it, they were just eating it up
insights.locator: The most frustrating part, she says

insights.claim: One Boulder seller's Etsy sales fell 30 percent in 2022 and 50 percent the following year as competitors, Alibaba drop-ship copies, and AI portraits proliferated, after she made over $40,000 on the platform in 2021.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Illustrates the speed of income collapse for individual makers, a leading indicator of seller churn.
insights.locator: In 2022, she says, her sales decreased by 30 percent

insights.claim: A UK illustrator on Etsy for 10 years says his sales fell 98 percent in a single year, attributing it to people using the platform as an easy way to make a quick buck with AI content.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Extreme individual declines suggest a broader exodus of the artisan supply that was Etsy's differentiator.
insights.quote: People have learned not to trust once-trusted sites like Etsy, because they are tired of seeing AI slop that is now infecting the site

insights.claim: AI-generated listings have spread beyond mugs and T-shirts into crocheting patterns, hermit crab pools, and medieval suits of armor, driven by online entrepreneurs promoting Etsy as a place for AI-driven side hustles.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Category-wide AI infiltration erodes the whole marketplace's handmade positioning, not just art niches.

insights.claim: Etsy officially decided in 2024 to keep allowing AI-generated art if sellers disclose it and barred selling custom AI prompts, but sellers say the disclosure mandate is not strictly enforced and the company is largely unresponsive to complaints.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: A disclosure rule without enforcement is effectively no rule, accelerating trust erosion and seller flight.

insights.claim: Etsy's VP of customer operations conceded that enforcing its AI-transparency standards is complex and that the company has a lot more work to do on detecting and removing listings that don't belong.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Management's own admission of enforcement weakness signals the problem persists near-term.
insights.quote: continuing to improve our ability to detect and remove listings that don't belong on Etsy
insights.locator: In response to a list of questions

insights.claim: Etsy's gross merchandise sales have declined since 2021, which analysts attribute to increased competition from Temu and Shein, and the company laid off 11 percent of its workforce in late 2023.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: GMS decline plus low-cost-import competition frames a structurally pressured marketplace despite stable revenue.
insights.locator: Since 2021, the company’s gross merchandise sales have declined

insights.claim: Etsy has roughly 5.6 million sellers and generated $2.8 billion in revenue last year, a 2.7 percent increase from 2024, showing revenue growth even as seller sentiment sours.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Revenue resilience masks supply-side discontent that could hollow out the platform's differentiation over time.
insights.locator: In recent years, however, the platform has largely maintained

insights.claim: A new crafts marketplace, Fybe, opens to vendors August 1 and explicitly bans AI-generated content and drop-shipping, relying on volunteer human moderators to guarantee products are human-made.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: A 'human-made only' positioning is emerging as a competitive wedge against incumbent marketplaces.
insights.locator: Competitors are emerging. Fybe, for instance

insights.claim: A departing seller who pivoted from Etsy to Shopify found sales disappointing, noting it is harder to sell on your own website than on community marketplaces.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Marketplace network effects still lock sellers in despite grievances, limiting churn to Etsy's revenue.

insights.claim: The rise of cheap AI content raises the existential question of whether handmade or crafted products have a viable future when consumers may not care about the difference and the AI version is cheaper and more accessible.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: If provenance premium erodes, the entire artisan-goods category faces demand risk, not just Etsy.
insights.locator: But given the rising popularity of AI slop

insights.claim: A seller frames art as a discretionary luxury that is among the first things cut amid economic uncertainty, tying handmade-goods demand to the broader consumer spending cycle.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Handmade-goods demand is cyclically exposed, compounding the secular AI threat.
insights.quote: Face it, art is a luxury, and with all this economic uncertainty right now, it tends to be one of the last things people want to spend money on
